content.explanation:
🚦 Where 'Real Logic' Begins

Everything up to this point — variables, operators — has been about storing and comparing values. Conditionals are where your code starts making genuine DECISIONS: 'IF this is true, do this; OTHERWISE, do something else.' This is the foundation of all programming logic, and it's exactly what comparison/logical operators from last lesson were building toward.

🚥 The Traffic Light Analogy

Think of a traffic light's logic: IF the light is green, go. ELSE IF the light is yellow, slow down. ELSE (meaning it must be red), stop. Notice this checks conditions in ORDER, top to bottom, and only ONE branch actually runs — once a condition matches, the rest are skipped entirely. This is exactly how JavaScript's if/else if/else structure behaves.

🧠 Why Order Matters in Conditionals

JavaScript checks conditions from TOP to BOTTOM and stops at the FIRST one that's true — it never checks the rest after that, even if they'd also technically be true. This means the ORDER you write your conditions in can genuinely change your program's behavior, especially when conditions could overlap. Getting comfortable thinking through this order is a core programming skill, not just a JavaScript quirk.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧱 Basic if Statement

const age = 20;

if (age >= 18) {
  console.log("You are an adult.");
}
  • if — the keyword that starts a conditional
  • (age >= 18) — the CONDITION, wrapped in parentheses. This must evaluate to true or false (remember comparison operators from Lesson 23)
  • { } — curly braces wrap the code that runs ONLY IF the condition is true

If age were 15 instead, the condition would be false, and the code inside the curly braces would simply be SKIPPED — nothing inside would run at all.

🧱 if / else — Two-Way Decisions

const age = 15;

if (age >= 18) {
  console.log("You are an adult.");
} else {
  console.log("You are a minor.");
}

else defines what runs if the if condition is FALSE. Exactly one of these two blocks will run — never both, never neither.

🧱 if / else if / else — Multiple Possible Outcomes

const score = 75;

if (score >= 90) {
  console.log("Grade: A");
} else if (score >= 70) {
  console.log("Grade: B");
} else if (score >= 50) {
  console.log("Grade: C");
} else {
  console.log("Grade: F");
}

This checks each condition in strict order. For score = 75: is it >= 90? No. Is it >= 70? YES — so "Grade: B" prints, and JavaScript stops checking further, completely skipping the remaining else-if and else blocks, even though 75 is technically also >= 50.

🧱 Combining with Logical Operators

const hasTicket = true;
const isVIP = false;

if (hasTicket && isVIP) {
  console.log("VIP entrance");
} else if (hasTicket) {
  console.log("General entrance");
} else {
  console.log("Access denied");
}

This combines a comparison-style condition with the && operator from Lesson 23, showing exactly why understanding logical operators first makes conditionals far more powerful.
content.common_mistakes:
⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes

  • Using = instead of === inside a condition — if (age = 18) assigns 18 to age instead of comparing, which is a serious, sneaky bug.
  • Forgetting the parentheses around the condition — if age >= 18 { } without parentheses causes an error.
  • Writing conditions in the wrong order — e.g. checking score >= 50 BEFORE score >= 90, which would incorrectly catch high scores in the wrong, too-broad bracket.
  • Forgetting that only ONE branch runs — beginners sometimes expect multiple matching else-if blocks to all execute, but only the FIRST true one does.

mini_challenge.instruction:
🎯 Challenge:

1. In your script, create a number variable called temperature
2. Write an if / else if / else that logs "Hot" if above 30, "Warm" if above 20, otherwise "Cold"
3. Use console.log() to print: conditionals make decisions in code
4. Below the script, add a paragraph (visible on the page) saying: conditionals make decisions in code
5. Run and verify your code

quiz_engine.question: In an if / else if / else chain, how many branches actually run?
options: All branches whose conditions are true
Only the FIRST branch whose condition is true — the rest are skipped
Always all of them, regardless of conditions
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 JavaScript checks conditions top to bottom and stops at the first true one — the remaining else-if/else blocks are skipped entirely, even if they'd also technically be true.

takeaways: if/else lets your code make decisions based on conditions
Conditions go inside parentheses and must evaluate to true or false
Only ONE branch in an if/else if/else chain runs — the first true one
Conditions are checked top to bottom — order can change behavior
Always use === (not =) inside conditions to compare, not assign

cheat_sheet.if (condition) { }: Runs code only if condition is true
cheat_sheet.else { }: Runs if the if condition was false
cheat_sheet.else if (condition) { }: Checks another condition if the previous ones were false
cheat_sheet.Execution Order: Top to bottom — stops at the first true condition
